name: neutrino-architecture-contract
purpose: Define architecture in one place and enforce implementation conformance for a composable AI service platform.
principles: All infrastructure integrations must be implemented as adapters behind stable ports.
Application code imports schema and ports, not concrete infrastructure adapters.
app_id is the canonical app identity key across control-plane and runtime surfaces.
Apps are the developer-facing product artifact; apps publish objects, actions, views, and visibility rules.
Services are reusable implementation units behind actions, not first-class product artifacts.
Service identity must be package-style, namespaced, and versioned.
Service definitions are product/platform manifests, not Docker-like container packaging specs.
The minimum internal kernel is Workspace, Org, Project, Actor, Group, Identity, Grant, App, Object, Action, Service, Policy, Schema, Binding, Execution, and Record.
File-defined resources must round-trip with builder UIs instead of creating hidden app-specific resource structures.
Auth, storage, memory, artifacts, catalog, invocation, observability, and policy are platform core concerns.
Postgres is the durable system of record for core state; vector and realtime systems can be swapped.
pgvector is the first vector implementation, not a permanent platform assumption.
ObjectStorage owns artifact bytes; Postgres owns artifact metadata, permissions, and references.
The initial auth service must provide a real login surface and remain swappable toward Ory Kratos and SSO.
Deployment must remain push-to-deploy through CI workflows and IaC-managed environments.
Terraform-managed infrastructure must use a shared remote state backend instead of machine-local state.
The frontend design system is mandatory and built on shared shadcn/ui-based primitives and tokens.

deployment.apiContainer: Portable to App Runner through a standard Docker runtime contract.

designSystem.rule: Frontend features consume shared tokens and primitives instead of ad hoc page-level systems.

productVocabulary: Workspace is the top-level collaboration and billing/security boundary.
Org is a publisher, customer, vendor, or legal/business entity inside a workspace.
Project groups app installations and runtime work inside a workspace.
Actor is anything that can perform an action: a human, service account, app, or system process.
Group is the Unix-style collection primitive for teams, departments, customers, environments, or cohorts.
Identity links an actor to a login, SSO account, API key, or service credential.
Grant records what an actor or group can access.
rules: Do not model member, team_member, role, or permission_set as first-cut schema primitives.
Represent team and org structure through groups plus grants unless a concrete use case proves a separate table is needed.
Support SSO through identity records linked to actors, not by special-casing users in product schema.
Visibility governs app, action, object, view, and service exposure: internal, public, or explicitly allowed workspaces/orgs/groups/actors.

userFacingVocabulary: Objects represent app-defined things such as tickets, documents, accounts, or messages.
Actions mutate or retrieve app-backed data and may be exposed through MCP tools.
Views render objects with app-defined display metadata where the host supports it.
Services implement reusable work behind actions.
Policies control who and what is allowed.

internalKernel.name: App
internalKernel.definition: Versioned product package published by an org/workspace that exposes objects, actions, views, and visibility rules.

internalKernel.name: Object
internalKernel.definition: Small app-defined representation of a thing, with schema and optional display metadata.

internalKernel.name: Action
internalKernel.definition: App-defined callable operation that can use handlers or reusable services to read or mutate objects.

internalKernel.name: Service
internalKernel.definition: Versioned reusable implementation unit that can be installed, invoked by actions, donated, and governed.

internalKernel.name: Policy
internalKernel.definition: Identity, auth, authorization, scope, data boundary, approval, retention, sharing, and audit rules.

internalKernel.name: Schema
internalKernel.definition: Contracts for inputs, outputs, events, records, stored objects, and service configuration.

internalKernel.name: Binding
internalKernel.definition: Concrete resolution of a provider, tool, resource, or service dependency in a workspace, project, environment, app, or service scope.

internalKernel.name: Execution
internalKernel.definition: A single durable unit of work performed by a service.

internalKernel.name: Record
internalKernel.definition: Append-only typed JSONL fact emitted during execution.

serviceIdentity.workspaceNamespaceRule: Workspace and company namespaces such as @acme/* identify company-owned services that can be installed, reused, and governed.
serviceIdentity.sharedServiceRule: Durable shared services should generally use owner or workspace namespaces rather than app namespaces.
serviceIdentity.privateServiceRule: App-private generated services may exist, but must be explicitly marked private or project-scoped.

serviceDefinition.containerPackagingBoundary: Service manifests do not currently define Docker or OCI images, compose files, container entrypoints, exposed ports, sidecars, or healthchecks. If container/image execution is needed later, it belongs under implementation/runtime/binding details behind Service, not in the core product model.

subordinateConcepts.name: Harness
subordinateConcepts.role: Service/runtime implementation detail or operational scaffold.

subordinateConcepts.name: Eval
subordinateConcepts.role: Policy-attached check or execution/record validation result.

subordinateConcepts.name: Trace
subordinateConcepts.role: Structured records attached to an execution.

subordinateConcepts.name: Artifact
subordinateConcepts.role: Input, output, or storage reference attached to an execution.

subordinateConcepts.name: Capability/action/operation
subordinateConcepts.role: Avoid as primary nouns; at most technical service interface details.

serviceModel.recipeBoundary: Recipes, starters, and templates are deferred scaffolding conveniences, not first-class product artifacts, exposed services, or core runtime resources.
recordAndStorageModel: Append-only JSONL record streams capture canonical chronological facts.
Postgres owns relational indexes, metadata, execution status, service/package metadata, policy decisions, binding snapshots, and operational source of truth.
Blob/ObjectStorage owns large inputs, outputs, transcripts, files, traces, and generated artifacts.
Vector indexes may cover selected records/artifacts only and must inherit policy metadata from source records/artifacts.
Materialized views may power service history, usage, cost, eval outcome, audit, and product UI summaries.

ports.swapRule: App and domain layers depend on LanguageModelProvider only. The term model is reserved for LLM and AI model references.
Embedding generation is isolated behind EmbeddingProvider before records reach indexes.
VectorIndex is selected by configuration, not by app imports.
ObjectStorage stores artifact bytes; ArtifactRepository stores canonical metadata in core persistence.
Realtime protocol details stay inside collaboration adapters.
Provider APIs are abstracted by RepoProvider.
Authentication providers are swappable behind IdentityProvider, with Ory Kratos and SSO as later adapter targets.
Session issuance and validation remain provider-agnostic through SessionManager.
Policy decisions are isolated behind PolicyEngine, with OpenFGA as a later adapter target.
OAuth app registration and credential lifecycle run through OAuthClientCatalog.
Capability publishing, lifecycle, and discovery are mediated by CapabilityCatalog.

Repository interfaces describe canonical core persistence; Postgres-backed core implementations are selected by CORE_DATABASE_URL or explicit runtime configuration, with in-memory repositories retained for local bootstrap fallback.
